text: No portions of this operation were edited out. It was played at four times the regular speed, however. The objective is to show that this approach does not add excessive operative time. This was performed in twenty-four minutes.
The surgeon removes omental adhesions from the anterior abdominal wall, revealing a ventral incisional hernia at the umbilical trocar site, estimated to be approximately two centimeters in diameter.
A robotic approach may seem like overkill in this situation. However, the final repair is a superior result for many reasons.

The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap, revealing herniated preperitoneal adipose tissue protruding through the defect. This tissue is visible as it is manipulated to prevent future herniation and alleviate potential pain or bulging.
The surgeon exposes the full defect and estimates its size, then extends the preperitoneal pocket circumferentially to accommodate a 6.4 cm piece of mesh.
The surgeon separates the peritoneal lining from the posterior rectus fascia, preparing the fascial layer to incorporate the mesh securely.

The surgeon identifies the hernia sac as a small protrusion on the peritoneal layer, with its contents inverted back into the abdominal cavity. The preperitoneal pocket is expanded to accommodate the mesh, and the assistant hands over the suture material and mesh for placement.

The surgeon closes the hernia defect using a running technique with an absorbable barbed suture, spacing the sutures approximately one centimeter apart to evenly distribute tension and reduce the risk of seroma formation.

Seromas can be bothersome and can even become infected, compromising the integrity of the mesh. I tend to prefer placing all sutures before tightening them down and closing the defect. I think it is helpful to keep the perspective of the defect in sight while placing sutures. However, this is simply a personal preference.

The surgeon adds four additional throws to the barbed suture to maintain tension, then revisits the suture line, ending at the center of the hernia. The final suture pass is integrated into the mesh fixation process.
The surgeon circumferentially sutures the mesh to the posterior fascial wall using an absorbable barbed suture, ensuring the mesh remains in apposition during the healing process.

There are many benefits to this operative approach that are shown in this video. First, the herniated contents are completely reduced back into the abdominal space. Patients appreciate a better result when this is done. Second, the hernia defect is closed primarily, preventing the hernia from becoming larger over time and reducing the potential for seroma formation.

The surgeon fixates the mesh to the robust rectus fascia using sutures, ensuring stability and preventing migration. The peritoneal space is then completely closed over the mesh, concealing it from the intestine to prevent adhesions and potential bowel obstructions.

A benefit that is not seen here is the location and the size of the incisions required for this operation. This is done through three separate eight millimeter incisions that are placed on the side of the abdominal wall, far away from the location of the mesh. Mesh infections can be a very serious complication. Placing the incisions far away from the mesh reduces the risk that a skin infection would result in a mesh infection.
The surgeon completes the operation by removing all suture material from the abdomen, indicating the end of the procedure.
